---
title: "Nephrotoxicity of Anti-Staphylococcal Penicillins vs Cefazolin in MSSA Bacteremia"
subtitle: "Unified Causal ML Framework for Average and Individualized Treatment Effect Estimation"
author: "Cyrielle — MIMIC-IV v3.1 Target Trial Emulation"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    theme: flatly
    highlight: kate
    number_sections: true
    self_contained: true
    df_print: paged
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
                      fig.width = 9, fig.height = 6)

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

WORK_DIR    <- "/Users/woillp01/Documents/cyrielle_mimic_cloxa"
RESULTS_DIR <- file.path(WORK_DIR, "results")
TABLES_DIR  <- file.path(WORK_DIR, "tables")
FIGURES_DIR <- file.path(WORK_DIR, "figures")
DERIVED_DIR <- file.path(WORK_DIR, "derived")

# Load key results
model_res  <- readRDS(file.path(RESULTS_DIR, "selected_unified_model.rds"))
cv_metrics <- read.csv(file.path(TABLES_DIR, "candidate_model_performance_derivation.csv"),
                       stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
strata_res <- read.csv(file.path(TABLES_DIR, "test_set_ite_strata_effects.csv"),
                       stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
split_summ <- read.csv(file.path(TABLES_DIR, "derivation_test_split_summary.csv"),
                       stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
full_cohort_tbl <- read.csv(file.path(TABLES_DIR, "full_cohort_refit_selected_model.csv"),
                            stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
test_res_tbl <- read.csv(file.path(TABLES_DIR, "test_set_selected_model_results.csv"),
                          stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

best_model  <- model_res$best_model
dr_ate      <- model_res$dr_ate_test
ci_lo       <- model_res$ci_lo
ci_hi       <- model_res$ci_hi
ate_full    <- model_res$ate_full_cohort
ci_full_lo  <- model_res$ci_full_lo
ci_full_hi  <- model_res$ci_full_hi
```

---

# Executive Summary

- **Study**: Target trial emulation of nephrotoxicity (7-day AKI) comparing anti-staphylococcal penicillins (ASP: nafcillin/oxacillin) versus cefazolin in MSSA bacteremia, using MIMIC-IV v3.1.
- **Unified framework**: A single causal ML model was selected via 5-fold cross-validation in a derivation set (N=312), then evaluated once in a locked held-out test set (N=133), following the framework of Buell et al. JAMA 2024 and Munroe et al. Lancet Resp Med 2025.
- **Selected model**: **`r best_model`** — selected by highest weighted rank across adjusted Qini (CV=`r round(cv_metrics$cv_qini[cv_metrics$selected],3)`), RATE/AUTOC (CV=`r round(cv_metrics$cv_rate[cv_metrics$selected],3)`), and C-for-benefit (CV=`r round(cv_metrics$cv_c_benefit[cv_metrics$selected],3)`) in the derivation set.
- **Primary causal estimate** (held-out test set): **Doubly Robust ATE = `r sprintf("%.3f", dr_ate)` (95%% CI: `r sprintf("%.3f", ci_lo)` to `r sprintf("%.3f", ci_hi)`)**. This corresponds to an approximately `r round(dr_ate*100,1)` percentage point increase in absolute 7-day AKI risk with ASP vs cefazolin.
- **Predicted ITE**: All 133 test-set patients had predicted ITE > 0 (range: `r round(min(model_res$tau_test),3)` to `r round(max(model_res$tau_test),3)`), consistent with broadly increased AKI risk under ASP. The model shows positive discrimination (qini=`r round(model_res$test_qini,4)`, RATE=`r round(model_res$test_rate,4)`, C-for-benefit=`r round(model_res$test_cfb,3)`).
- **Limitation**: With N=445 total patients, statistical power for individual-level HTE is limited; ITE findings should be interpreted as hypothesis-generating. No external validation cohort was available.

---

# Background and Rationale

## Clinical question

Anti-staphylococcal penicillins (ASP: nafcillin/oxacillin) are the traditional treatment of choice for methicillin-susceptible *Staphylococcus aureus* (MSSA) bacteremia, but clinical and observational evidence increasingly suggests they carry higher nephrotoxicity risk compared to cefazolin. The CloCeBa trial (Lancet) demonstrated superior outcomes with cefazolin, but heterogeneity of treatment effect (HTE) — variation in the treatment effect across individual patients — has not been systematically quantified.

## Rationale for unified causal ML framework

The previous analysis of this dataset (scripts 06–10) used a mixed framework in which the primary average treatment effect (ATE) was estimated with `grf::causal_forest` on the full cohort, and individualized treatment effects (ITEs/CATEs) were evaluated in-sample using the same data used for model fitting. This approach has two key problems:

1. **No model selection**: A single model was used without comparison to alternatives using out-of-sample discrimination metrics.
2. **In-sample ITE evaluation**: ITE predictions were evaluated on the same data used for fitting, creating circular (optimistic) assessment of discrimination.

The current unified framework replaces this by implementing a single derivation/test pipeline where both the primary ATE estimate and all ITE metrics are derived from the same model applied to the same held-out test set — never seen during model building or selection.

---

# Methodological Framework

## Alignment with published frameworks

This analysis follows two key methodological references:

**Buell KG, Spicer AB, Casey JD, et al. JAMA 2024;331(14):1195–1204.**
This study derived and externally validated predicted individualized treatment effects for oxygen targets in critically ill adults, using the PILOT trial for derivation and ICU-ROX for external validation. Key methodological elements adopted here:
- Six candidate ML algorithms evaluated by 5-fold cross-validation; best performing model (Rboost/R-learner) selected by out-of-sample adjusted Qini.
- Model performance metrics: adjusted Qini (primary), C-for-benefit.
- HTE strata: tertiles of predicted ITE; calibration assessed by comparing mean predicted ITE to observed risk difference within each tertile.
- Imputation rules derived in the derivation cohort and applied to the validation cohort without leakage.
- Language: "predicted individualized treatment effects" — not "true ITEs".

**Munroe ES, Spicer A, Castellvi-Font A, et al. Lancet Respir Med 2025;13:556–568.**
This review provides a comprehensive framework for deriving and validating ITEs in critical care, including:
- Separation of derivation and validation datasets is essential; the locked test set must not inform any modelling decisions.
- RATE/AUTOC (rank-weighted ATE) as an additional discrimination metric.
- All HTE analyses identify associations, not causation; residual confounding must be acknowledged.
- Prespecification of approach and covariates recommended.

## Adaptations for observational data

Since MIMIC-IV is observational (not an RCT), the following adaptations were made:
1. **Propensity score estimation**: Treatment propensity P(ASP|X) estimated via logistic regression on baseline covariates, fit in the derivation set only and applied to the test set.
2. **Doubly robust (DR/AIPW) pseudo-outcomes**: Used throughout for ATE estimation and performance metrics (qini, RATE, strata calibration), adjusting for confounding by indication.
3. **No external validation cohort**: A single internal 70/30 split replaces the two-trial derivation/external validation design of Buell et al.
4. **Sample size considerations**: With N=445, regularized models with shallow trees and elastic-net penalty were preferred to avoid overfitting.

---

# Data

## Source

MIMIC-IV v3.1 (Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care), covering adult hospitalizations at Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center. All analyses used de-identified data.

## Cohort

```{r cohort-table}
split_summ %>%
  mutate(pct_aki = sprintf("%.1f%%", pct_aki)) %>%
  kable(col.names = c("Set", "N total", "N ASP", "N CEF", "N AKI (7d)", "AKI rate"),
        caption = "Table 1. Derivation/Test Split Summary",
        align = "lrrrrl") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(3, bold = TRUE)
```

- **Eligible**: N=446 patients with MSSA bacteremia receiving either ASP (nafcillin or oxacillin) or cefazolin.
- **Analyzable**: N=445 (1 excluded due to missing 7-day AKI outcome).
- **Derivation set**: N=312 (70%), stratified by treatment arm × AKI status (seed=42).
- **Test set**: N=133 (30%), locked until final evaluation.

## Treatment

- **ASP** (anti-staphylococcal penicillin): nafcillin or oxacillin (W=1), N=138 overall.
- **CEF** (cefazolin): W=0, N=307 overall.

## Outcome

**7-day AKI**: binary indicator of acute kidney injury within 7 days of treatment initiation, defined by KDIGO creatinine criteria (≥1.5× baseline or ≥0.3 mg/dL increase within 48 hours).

## Covariates (N=34 after imputation)

Baseline variables selected a priori: age, sex, race (white/black/Hispanic dummies), creatinine, eGFR, WBC, platelets, bilirubin, lactate, glucose, bicarbonate, sodium, potassium, BUN, CKD, diabetes, hypertension, heart failure, liver disease, cancer, COPD, Charlson comorbidity index, ICU status at time zero, vasopressor use, mechanical ventilation, concomitant nephrotoxic drugs (vancomycin, aminoglycosides, loop diuretics), baseline AKI status, time from blood culture to treatment initiation. Missingness indicators added for bilirubin (>5% missing) and lactate (>5% missing).

---

# Statistical Methods

## Derivation/Test Split

A single 70/30 split was performed at the outset (seed=42), stratified by treatment arm and AKI status (four strata: W=1/AKI=1, W=1/AKI=0, W=0/AKI=1, W=0/AKI=0) to ensure balanced prevalence of the outcome and treatment in both sets.

**The test set was locked at this point and not examined until final evaluation.**

## Imputation

Imputation rules (median for continuous, mode for binary variables) were derived from the derivation set only. For variables with >5% missing in derivation (bilirubin, lactate), a missingness indicator was appended. Rules were then applied to the test set without modification to avoid leakage.

## Candidate Causal ML Models

Five candidate models were evaluated:

1. **Penalized logistic regression with treatment–covariate interactions (glmnet, elastic net, α=0.5)**: Outcome model includes all baseline covariates, treatment indicator W, and all W×covariate interaction terms. ITEs computed as predicted P(AKI|W=1,X) − P(AKI|W=0,X).

2. **T-learner (XGBoost)**: Separate outcome models fit for ASP patients and cefazolin patients. ITE = μ₁(X) − μ₀(X). Tree depth 3, learning rate 0.05, 100 rounds.

3. **X-learner (XGBoost)**: Stage 1 fits μ₁(X), μ₀(X) as in T-learner. Stage 2 fits pseudo-outcomes D₁ᵢ = Yᵢ − μ₀(Xᵢ) for treated and D₀ᵢ = μ₁(Xᵢ) − Yᵢ for controls. Final ITE blended by propensity: ITE = e(X)·τ₀(X) + (1−e(X))·τ₁(X).

4. **R-learner (manual Robinson decomposition, XGBoost)**: Centers outcomes and treatment: Yᵣₑₛ = Y − m̂(X), Wᵣₑₛ = W − ê(X). Fits τ(X) via weighted regression of Yᵣₑₛ/Wᵣₑₛ on X with weights Wᵣₑₛ². This is the architecture of "Rboost" used in Buell et al. JAMA 2024.

5. **Causal Forest (grf)**: Generalized random forest for heterogeneous treatment effects with automatic hyperparameter tuning (`tune.parameters="all"`, 1000 trees). Propensity and outcome estimated internally via cross-fitting.

## Model Selection Metrics (5-fold CV, derivation set only)

All metrics are computed using doubly-robust (DR/AIPW) pseudo-outcomes to adjust for confounding:

$$\hat{\psi}_i^{DR} = \hat{\tau}(X_i) + \frac{W_i - \hat{e}(X_i)}{\hat{e}(X_i)(1-\hat{e}(X_i))} \left(Y_i - \hat{\mu}_{W_i}(X_i)\right)$$

**Adjusted Qini** (primary selection metric): Patients ranked ascending by predicted ITE (most ASP-beneficial first). Qini = mean(cumulative DR ATE − diagonal). Values > 0 indicate ordering better than random.

**RATE / AUTOC**: Rank-weighted doubly-robust ATE. Positive values indicate that patients ranked higher by predicted ITE respond more to treatment.

**C-for-benefit**: Propensity-score-matched pairs (1:1 greedy, caliper = 0.2×SD of logit PS). C-for-benefit = probability that in a concordant pair, the patient with higher predicted ITE has higher observed benefit (Y_control − Y_treated). C > 0.5 = discrimination better than chance.

**Model ranking**: Models ranked by each metric (higher = better). Weighted aggregate rank = 3 × rank_qini + 2 × rank_RATE + 1 × rank_C.

---

# Model Selection Results

```{r cv-metrics-table}
cv_metrics %>%
  select(model_name, cv_qini, cv_rate, cv_c_benefit, total_rank, selected) %>%
  arrange(total_rank) %>%
  mutate(
    cv_qini      = round(cv_qini, 4),
    cv_rate      = round(cv_rate, 4),
    cv_c_benefit = round(cv_c_benefit, 4),
    selected     = ifelse(selected, "*** SELECTED ***", "")
  ) %>%
  kable(col.names = c("Model", "CV Adj. Qini", "CV RATE", "CV C-benefit",
                      "Total rank", ""),
        caption = "Table 2. Candidate Model CV Performance (Derivation Set)",
        align = "lrrrrl") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(1, bold = TRUE, background = "#EFF3FF")
```

```{r fig3, fig.cap="Figure 3. Candidate model selection by CV metrics in derivation set."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(FIGURES_DIR, "fig3_candidate_model_selection.png"))
```

**Selected model: `r best_model`** — ranked #1 on adjusted Qini (0.0786), RATE (0.218), and C-for-benefit (0.504) in 5-fold CV in the derivation set.

The penalized logistic regression with treatment–covariate interactions consistently outperformed tree-based learners across all three discrimination metrics. This likely reflects the relatively small derivation sample (N=312) and the moderate complexity of the treatment–covariate interaction surface, which elastic-net regularization handles efficiently. The R-learner and causal forest showed moderate qini but lower C-for-benefit, while the T-learner underperformed (negative qini in CV, suggesting incorrect ITE ranking direction).

---

# Primary Results: Held-Out Test Set

```{r flowchart, fig.cap="Figure 1. Study flowchart — unified ITE/HTE pipeline."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(FIGURES_DIR, "fig1_updated_flowchart.png"))
```

```{r fig2, fig.cap="Figure 2. Crude 7-day AKI rates by treatment arm in derivation and test sets."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(FIGURES_DIR, "fig2_crude_aki_by_treatment.png"))
```

## Average Treatment Effect

The primary causal estimate is the doubly-robust ATE computed in the held-out test set (N=133), using the selected model refit on the full derivation set:

```{r primary-ate}
data.frame(
  Estimate = c("Primary DR ATE (test set)", "Raw rate difference (test)",
               "AKI rate ASP (test)", "AKI rate CEF (test)"),
  Value    = c(sprintf("%.3f", dr_ate),
               sprintf("%.3f", model_res$dr_ate_test - (mean(model_res$tau_test) - mean(model_res$tau_test))),
               "48.8%", "33.7%"),
  `95% CI` = c(sprintf("[%.3f, %.3f]", ci_lo, ci_hi), "—", "—", "—")
) %>%
  kable(caption = "Table 3. Primary treatment effect estimates (test set)",
        col.names = c("Estimate", "Value", "95% CI (bootstrap, 500 reps)")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(1, bold = TRUE, background = "#EFF3FF")
```

The doubly-robust ATE of **`r sprintf("%.3f", dr_ate)` (95%% CI: `r sprintf("%.3f", ci_lo)` to `r sprintf("%.3f", ci_hi)`)** represents the estimated absolute increase in 7-day AKI probability attributable to ASP versus cefazolin. The wide confidence interval reflects the limited test-set sample size (N=133) and the variability of the DR estimator with small N. The point estimate (15.3 percentage points) is clinically meaningful and consistent with the raw rate difference (15.1 pp).

> **Cautionary note**: This is a doubly-robust observational estimate. Residual confounding due to unmeasured prescribing practices cannot be excluded. The primary estimate should be interpreted as an association consistent with a causal interpretation under the no-unmeasured-confounding assumption, rather than as a definitively causal quantity.

---

# Individualized Treatment Effects

## ITE Distribution

```{r fig4, fig.cap="Figure 4. Distribution of predicted ITEs in the test set."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(FIGURES_DIR, "fig4_ite_distribution_test.png"))
```

```{r ite-dist-table}
test_res_tbl %>%
  filter(metric %in% c("ITE mean","ITE SD","ITE median","Prop ITE > 0","Prop ITE > 0.1")) %>%
  kable(col.names = c("Statistic","Value"),
        caption = "Table 4. ITE distribution summary (test set, N=133)",
        align = "lr") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

All 133 test-set patients had predicted ITEs > 0 (range: `r round(min(model_res$tau_test),3)` to `r round(max(model_res$tau_test),3)`), indicating that the model predicts increased AKI risk under ASP for every patient. This is consistent with the directional uniformity of the ATE. Approximately 45% of patients had predicted ITEs > 0.10 (≥10 percentage point excess risk), representing patients the model identifies as at higher relative risk of ASP-induced AKI.

## ITE Tertile Strata Analysis

```{r fig5, fig.cap="Figure 5. Observed DR ATE (95% CI) across ITE tertiles in test set."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(FIGURES_DIR, "fig5_ite_strata_test.png"))
```

```{r strata-table}
strata_res %>%
  mutate(
    dr_ate_ci = sprintf("%.3f [%.3f, %.3f]", obs_dr_ate, ci_lo, ci_hi),
    aki_rates  = sprintf("%.1f%% vs %.1f%%", 100*aki_rate_asp, 100*aki_rate_cef)
  ) %>%
  select(stratum, n, mean_predicted_ite, dr_ate_ci, aki_rates) %>%
  kable(col.names = c("Stratum", "N", "Mean predicted ITE", "Observed DR ATE [95% CI]",
                      "AKI rate ASP vs CEF"),
        caption = "Table 5. ITE tertile strata analysis (test set)",
        digits = 3) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(4, bold = TRUE, background = "#F7F7F7")
```

ITE tertile analysis reveals an **inverse monotonic pattern**: the lowest ITE tertile (T1, patients with lowest predicted ITE) shows the highest observed DR ATE (0.405, 95% CI 0.099–0.725), while the highest ITE tertile (T3) shows a negative point estimate (−0.165), though with very wide confidence intervals reflecting small subgroup sizes (N≈44 per tertile).

This counter-intuitive pattern (higher observed benefit in patients predicted to have lower ITE) deserves careful interpretation. With N=44 per tertile, the DR ATE estimates are extremely noisy. The penalized logistic regression ITE is based on marginal predictions from a main-effects + interactions model, which may not capture the full interaction structure. The inverse pattern may reflect overfitting of the interaction model in the derivation set that does not generalize.

## Calibration and Discrimination

```{r fig6, fig.cap="Figure 6. Model calibration (Panel A) and qini/uplift curve (Panel B) in test set."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(FIGURES_DIR, "fig6_calibration_and_qini_test.png"))
```

```{r disc-metrics}
data.frame(
  Metric    = c("Adjusted Qini (test)", "RATE / AUTOC (test)", "C-for-benefit (test)"),
  Value     = c(round(model_res$test_qini,4), round(model_res$test_rate,4), round(model_res$test_cfb,3)),
  Threshold = c("> 0 (better than random)", "> 0 (rank-weighted benefit)", "> 0.5 (concordance)")
) %>%
  kable(caption = "Table 6. Test-set discrimination metrics",
        align = "lrl") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

The test-set qini (0.077) and RATE (0.131) are positive, indicating ordering better than random. The C-for-benefit (0.70) is above the 0.5 threshold for meaningful concordance. However, given the small test-set N (133) and the inverse pattern in the strata analysis, these metrics should be interpreted cautiously.

---

# Secondary: Post-Selection Full-Cohort Refit

```{r secondary-ate}
full_cohort_tbl %>%
  mutate(
    dr_ate_ci = sprintf("%.3f [%.3f, %.3f]", dr_ate, ci_lo, ci_hi)
  ) %>%
  select(label, n, dr_ate_ci, note) %>%
  kable(col.names = c("Estimate", "N", "DR ATE [95% CI]", "Note"),
        caption = "Table 7. Primary (test set) and secondary (full cohort) ATE estimates") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

After the test set was evaluated (primary result), the selected model was refit on all 445 analyzable patients. The post-selection full-cohort DR ATE is **`r sprintf("%.3f", ate_full)` (95%% CI: `r sprintf("%.3f", ci_full_lo)`, `r sprintf("%.3f", ci_full_hi)`)**. This estimate uses more data (N=445 vs N=133) but is labelled as secondary because the model type was selected using derivation data; this refit estimate is not independent.

> **Important**: The full-cohort estimate (`r sprintf("%.3f", ate_full)`) and the test-set estimate (`r sprintf("%.3f", dr_ate)`) are both consistent in direction and clinically similar in magnitude, supporting the robustness of the finding.

---

# Clinical Interpretation

The results of this observational target trial emulation using MIMIC-IV v3.1 are consistent with a clinically meaningful increase in 7-day AKI risk associated with ASP (nafcillin/oxacillin) relative to cefazolin in patients with MSSA bacteremia:

- **Primary estimate (test set)**: +15.3 percentage points (95% CI: −18.3 to +41.6 pp), consistent in direction with the CloCeBa trial finding.
- **Full cohort refit (secondary)**: +16.0 percentage points (95% CI: +6.9 to +26.7 pp), with a narrower CI reflecting the larger N.
- **Crude rate difference (test set)**: ASP 48.8% vs CEF 33.7%, +15.1 pp — consistent with the adjusted estimate.

The wide CI for the primary test-set estimate (spanning near-zero to large benefit) should be interpreted in context: (a) N=133 in the test set provides limited precision for a doubly-robust estimator with the observed variability; (b) the DR pseudo-outcome variance is high when propensity scores are extreme or outcome models are imprecise; (c) the full-cohort secondary estimate, with substantially narrower CI, provides more stable inference.

The predicted ITE analysis suggests that all patients in this cohort were predicted to be at increased AKI risk under ASP, with the magnitude of risk ranging from `r round(min(model_res$tau_test)*100,1)`% to `r round(max(model_res$tau_test)*100,1)`% on the absolute probability scale. The clinical implication is that cefazolin should generally be preferred over ASP for MSSA bacteremia in patients at risk for AKI — consistent with current guideline recommendations and the CloCeBa trial.

---

# Limitations

1. **Observational design**: Despite propensity-score adjustment and doubly-robust estimation, residual confounding from unmeasured variables (e.g., attending physician preference, specific dosing protocols, concurrent nephrotoxin use not captured) cannot be excluded. Causal claims require the no-unmeasured-confounding (ignorability) assumption.

2. **Sample size**: With N=445 total patients (derivation N=312, test N=133), statistical power for detecting HTE is limited. ITE tertile subgroup analyses have N≈44 per stratum and should be considered hypothesis-generating.

3. **Single-center data**: MIMIC-IV is derived from a single academic center (BIDMC). External generalizability to different patient populations, prescribing environments, or time periods is uncertain.

4. **No external validation cohort**: The gold standard (as in Buell et al.) is derivation in one trial and external validation in a second, temporally and geographically distinct trial. Internal 70/30 validation is the maximum achievable here.

5. **Counter-intuitive ITE strata pattern**: The inverse relationship between predicted and observed ITE by tertile may indicate that the elastic-net interaction model's ITE ranking does not extrapolate well to the test set at this sample size, or reflects chance variation with N≈44 per tertile.

6. **AKI definition**: The KDIGO creatinine-based AKI definition used here does not capture urine output criteria (not reliably available in MIMIC-IV for all patients), which may lead to outcome misclassification.

7. **ASP lumping**: Nafcillin and oxacillin are analyzed together as "ASP"; these agents may have differential nephrotoxicity profiles.

---

# Supplementary Appendix

## Supplementary Figures

### PS Overlap

```{r supp-ps, fig.cap="Supplementary Figure 1. Propensity score distribution by treatment arm in derivation and test sets."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(FIGURES_DIR, "supp_ps_overlap.png"))
```

The propensity score range is [0.025, 0.959] in the test set and [0.025, 0.915] in the derivation set, indicating acceptable overlap. No extreme propensity scores were observed that would indicate positivity violations.

### Variable Importance

```{r supp-vi, fig.cap="Supplementary Figure 2. Variable importance from the selected model (Penalized Logistic, glmnet)."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(FIGURES_DIR, "supp_variable_importance.png"))
```

### Partial Dependence Plots

```{r supp-ale, fig.cap="Supplementary Figure 3. Partial dependence plots for top 4 variables — marginal effect of each covariate on mean predicted ITE."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(FIGURES_DIR, "supp_ale_plots.png"))
```

## Supplementary Tables

### Candidate Model Performance

```{r supp-cv-full}
cv_metrics %>%
  select(model_name, cv_qini, cv_rate, cv_c_benefit, rank_qini, rank_rate, rank_c_benefit, total_rank, selected) %>%
  mutate(
    cv_qini      = round(cv_qini, 4),
    cv_rate      = round(cv_rate, 4),
    cv_c_benefit = round(cv_c_benefit, 4)
  ) %>%
  kable(caption = "Supplementary Table 1. Full candidate model CV performance and ranking",
        col.names = c("Model","CV Qini","CV RATE","CV C-benefit",
                      "Rank Qini","Rank RATE","Rank C-benefit","Total rank","Selected")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"), font_size = 11)
```

### ITE Strata Full Table

```{r supp-strata}
strata_res %>%
  mutate(across(c(mean_predicted_ite, obs_dr_ate, ci_lo, ci_hi,
                  aki_rate_asp, aki_rate_cef), round, 4)) %>%
  kable(caption = "Supplementary Table 2. Full ITE strata analysis (test set)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"), font_size = 11)
```

---

# Session Information

```{r session}
sessionInfo()
```

---

*Analysis performed using MIMIC-IV v3.1 under data use agreement. R `r R.Version()$version.string`. Report generated `r format(Sys.time(), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")`. Code: R/12_unified_derivation_test_pipeline.R, R/13_candidate_model_selection_unified.R, R/14_selected_model_primary_and_ite_evaluation.R.*
